workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question models a savings account with monthly interest and withdrawals. The key is setting up the recurrence relation correctly and using the geometric series formula to find a closed form for Aₙ.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that each month follows the same pattern: first interest is added to the balance, then the withdrawal occurs. This creates a geometric series when we expand Aₙ. The critical observation for part (b) is that the sum of the geometric series equals 250(1.004ⁿ−1).

steps.title: Find A₁ and expand to A₂
steps.bodyLatex: At the end of month 1, after 0.4% interest is added, the balance is 10,000 × 1.004, then Jia withdraws M, giving A₁ = 10,000(1.004) − M. For month 2, this entire amount earns interest again, then another M is withdrawn: A₂ = (10,000(1.004) − M)(1.004) − M = 10,000(1.004)² − M(1.004) − M.

steps.title: Write Aₙ as a geometric series
steps.bodyLatex: Unfolding the recurrence n times gives: Aₙ = 10,000(1.004)ⁿ − M(1 + 1.004 + 1.004² + ... + 1.004ⁿ⁻¹). The term in brackets is a geometric series with first term 1, ratio 1.004, and n terms.

steps.title: Apply the geometric series formula
steps.bodyLatex: For a geometric series with first term a and ratio r ≠ 1, the sum is a(rⁿ−1)/(r−1). Here: 1 + 1.004 + ... + 1.004ⁿ⁻¹ = (1.004ⁿ − 1)/(1.004 − 1) = (1.004ⁿ − 1)/0.004 = 250(1.004ⁿ − 1). Substituting this into Aₙ and simplifying gives Aₙ = (10,000 − 250M)(1.004)ⁿ + 250M.

steps.title: Find M for exactly 100 withdrawals
steps.bodyLatex: For at least 100 withdrawals, the account must have positive balance after the 100th withdrawal: A₁₀₀ > 0. Using the formula from (b): (10,000 − 250M)(1.004)¹⁰⁰ + 250M > 0. Solving for M gives M < 10,000(1.004)¹⁰⁰ / [250((1.004)¹⁰⁰ − 1)] ≈ 121.527. The largest withdrawal to the nearest cent is $121.52.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to apply interest before the withdrawal each month, which reverses the order of terms. Always apply interest first, then withdraw.
Not simplifying the geometric series correctly — the denominator 0.004 equals 1.004 − 1, not forgetting to convert the percentage 0.4% to 0.004.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Quick check: if M = $40, then A₁ = 10,000(1.004) − 40 = $10,000, so the account never decreases. If M = $121.52, verify that A₁₀₀ is slightly positive using the formula.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Integrate the given rate function and apply the initial condition to find position; then use the period to count how many minima occur in the given time interval.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice the derivative is a sine function - integrating gives cosine. The swing's closest approach occurs at the minimum of x(t), which happens when the cosine term equals -1.

steps.title: Integrate the rate function
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(\frac{dx}{dt}=-1.5\pi\sin\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)\), integrate: \(x(t)=\int -1.5\pi\sin\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)\,dt\). Using \(\int \sin(ax)\,dx = -\frac{1}{a}\cos(ax)\), we get \(x(t)=1.2\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)+C\).

steps.title: Apply the initial condition
steps.bodyLatex: The swing is released from rest at distance 11.2 m, so \(x(0)=11.2\). Substituting: \(11.2=1.2\cos(0)+C=1.2+C\), giving \(C=10\). Thus \(x(t)=1.2\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)+10\).

steps.title: Find the period of motion
steps.bodyLatex: The period of \(\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)\) is \(T=\frac{2\pi}{5\pi/4}=\frac{8}{5}=1.6\) seconds. The swing oscillates between maximum distance 11.2 m and minimum distance 8.8 m.

steps.title: Count the closest approaches
steps.bodyLatex: The closest point (minimum of x) occurs when \(\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)=-1\), i.e., at \(t=\frac{T}{2}=0.8\) seconds, then every half-period (0.8 s). In 0 ≤ t ≤ 10: times are 0.8, 2.4, 4.0, 5.6, 7.2, 8.8 — giving 6 occurrences.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) \(x(t)=1.2\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)+10\). (b) The swing reaches the closest point 6 times in the first 10 seconds.
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the constant of integration C when integrating, or using the wrong initial value — always use x(0)=11.2 to find C.
Confusing the period (full oscillation) with the time between minima (half period). Minima occur every T/2 = 0.8 seconds, not every 1.6 seconds.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify: at t=0, x=1.2(1)+10=11.2 ✓. At t=0.8, x=1.2(-1)+10=8.8 m (closest). At t=10, the last minimum is at t=8.8, so 6 minima in total.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question involves finding parameters of an absolute value function from its graph, then determining when a line through the origin cuts the resulting V-shaped graph in two places.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice the vertex of the absolute value graph is the turning point at (6, 7), giving b and c directly. Then substitute either of the symmetric points (3, -5) or (9, -5) to find a. For part (b), visualise the line y = mx passing through the origin: it cuts twice when its slope lies between the critical slopes defined by the right branch and the line from origin to vertex.

steps.title: Identify the vertex
steps.bodyLatex: The graph shows an inverted V-shape with its peak at x = 6, y = 7. For the function $f(x) = a|x-b| + c$, the vertex is at $(b, c)$. Hence $b = 6$ and $c = 7$ directly from the diagram.

steps.title: Find a using a point on the graph
steps.bodyLatex: Using the point $(3, -5)$: substitute into $f(x) = a|x-6| + 7$. Since $|3-6| = 3$, we get $-5 = a(3) + 7$. Solving: $3a = -12$, so $a = -4$. Check: at $x = 9$, $|9-6| = 3$ gives $-4(3) + 7 = -5$ ✓

steps.title: Identify the critical slopes
steps.bodyLatex: The graph has two key boundary lines through the origin: (1) the line to the vertex $(6, 7)$ with slope $m_1 = \frac{7}{6}$, and (2) the right branch extended down to the x-axis (or thinking of its slope) with slope $m_2 = -4$.

steps.title: Determine the range for m
steps.bodyLatex: For $y = mx$ to cut the graph in two distinct places, the line must pass through the interior of the V-shape. This occurs when $-4 < m < \frac{7}{6}$. If $m = -4$ or $m = \frac{7}{6}$, the line is tangent (touches at one point). If $m \leq -4$ or $m \geq \frac{7}{6}$, it only touches one branch or misses the graph entirely.

commonMistakesLatex: Students often confuse the slopes: they might think the line cuts twice for $m > \frac{7}{6}$, but this gives only one intersection on the left branch. The key is that for two intersections, the line must pass between the right branch and the line through the origin and vertex.
Forgetting to check that m cannot equal the boundary values — at $m = -4$ or $m = \frac{7}{6}$ the line is tangent, giving only one intersection point, not two distinct ones.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify: at $m = 0$, the x-axis cuts through the two lower points at y = -5, giving two intersections. At $m = -4$, the line is parallel to the right branch and touches at the vertex only. At $m = \frac{7}{6}$, the line passes through the vertex and one lower point, giving one intersection.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Find where the curve has a tangent parallel to the given tangent (same gradient), then determine the exact y-coordinate using the integrated curve equation.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Parallel tangents share the same gradient. The given tangent y = x + 7 has gradient 1, so solve dy/dx = 1. Then integrate to find the full curve equation and use the known point T to find the constant of integration.

steps.title: Identify the gradient condition
steps.bodyLatex: The tangent at T has equation \(y = x + 7\). The gradient is the coefficient of \(x\), so \(\frac{dy}{dx} = 1\) at any point with a parallel tangent. Set the derivative equal to 1: \[3x^2 - 6x - 8 = 1\] This gives \(3x^2 - 6x - 9 = 0\).

steps.title: Solve for x-coordinates
steps.bodyLatex: Factor the quadratic: \(3x^2 - 6x - 9 = 3(x^2 - 2x - 3) = 3(x - 3)(x + 1) = 0\). Hence \(x = -1\) or \(x = 3\). The point \(T = (-1, 6)\) is already given, so the other parallel tangent is at \(x = 3\). This is point \(R\).

steps.title: Find the curve equation
steps.bodyLatex: Integrate the gradient function: \[y = \int (3x^2 - 6x - 8)\,dx = x^3 - 3x^2 - 8x + C\] Use point \(T(-1, 6)\) to find \(C\): \[6 = (-1)^3 - 3(-1)^2 - 8(-1) + C = -1 - 3 + 8 + C = 4 + C\] So \(C = 2\). The full curve is \(y = x^3 - 3x^2 - 8x + 2\).

steps.title: Find the y-coordinate of R
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \(x = 3\) into the curve equation: \[y = 3^3 - 3(3)^2 - 8(3) + 2 = 27 - 27 - 24 + 2 = -22\] Therefore \(R = (3, -22)\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to find the y-coordinate after solving for x. The derivative gives only the gradient (slope), not the actual point—you must integrate and use the known point to find the full curve equation.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify: \(\frac{dy}{dx}\) at \(x = 3\) equals \(3(3)^2 - 6(3) - 8 = 27 - 18 - 8 = 1\), confirming the tangent is parallel to the one at T.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: For a continuous PDF $f(x)=12x^2(1-x)$ on $[0,1]$, the mode is found by differentiating and setting $f'(x)=0$, giving $x=\frac23$. The CDF is obtained by integrating $f(x)$, giving $F(x)=4x^3-3x^4$ for $0\le x\le1$. To show the mode exceeds the median, we evaluate the CDF at the mode and find $F(\frac23)>0.5$, proving the median must be less than the mode.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice first that finding the mode requires calculus (the maximum of the PDF), while the CDF comes from straightforward integration. For part (c), notice that if $F(m)=0.5$ defines the median, and we find $F(\frac23)>0.5$, then $m$ must be less than $\frac23$ since $F(x)$ is increasing.

steps.title: Find the mode
steps.bodyLatex: Write $f(x)=12x^2-12x^3$ and differentiate: $f'(x)=24x-36x^2=12x(2-3x)$. Setting $f'(x)=0$ gives $x=0$ or $x=\frac23$. At the boundary $x=0$, $f(0)=0$. At the interior critical point $x=\frac23$, we have $f(\frac23)=12(\frac{4}{9})(\frac13)=\frac{16}{9}>0$, confirming a maximum. Thus the mode is $\frac23$.

steps.title: Evaluate CDF at the mode
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate $F(\frac23)=4(\frac{8}{27})-3(\frac{16}{81})=\frac{32}{27}-\frac{48}{81}=\frac{96-48}{81}=\frac{48}{81}=\frac{16}{27}\approx0.593$. This is greater than $0.5$, meaning more than $59\%$ of the probability lies below the mode.

steps.title: Conclude mode > median
steps.bodyLatex: Since the CDF is increasing and $F(\frac23)>0.5$, the median $m$ (where $F(m)=0.5$) must satisfy $m<\frac23$. Thus the mode $\frac23$ is greater than the median, as required.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) Mode $= \frac23$. (b) $F(x)=0$ for $x<0$, $F(x)=4x^3-3x^4$ for $0\le x\le1$, $F(x)=1$ for $x>1$. (c) Since $F(\frac23)=\frac{16}{27}>\frac12$, the median is less than $\frac23$, so the mode exceeds the median.
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to check boundary points when finding the mode. Here $x=0$ is a critical point but gives $f(0)=0$, not a maximum. Always verify the second derivative or compare function values.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the CDF integrates to 1: $\int_0^1(12x^2-12x^3)dx=[4x^3-3x^4]_0^1=4-3=1$, confirming a valid PDF.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Stationary points occur where \(f'(x)=0\), which for \(f(x)=e^{-x}\sin x\) happens when \(\cos x = \sin x\), giving \(x=\pi/4\) and \(x=5\pi/4\) in the interval.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Apply the product rule to find \(f'(x)\). Since \(e^{-x}>0\) for all \(x\), the stationary points come from solving \(\cos x = \sin x\). The graph can then be sketched using these stationary points and the zeros of \(f(x)\).

steps.title: Differentiate using the product rule
steps.bodyLatex: For \(f(x)=e^{-x}\sin x\), apply the product rule: \(f'(x) = (e^{-x})'\sin x + e^{-x}(\sin x)'\). Since \((e^{-x})' = -e^{-x}\) and \((\sin x)' = \cos x\), we get \(f'(x) = -e^{-x}\sin x + e^{-x}\cos x = e^{-x}(\cos x - \sin x)\).

steps.title: Find where f'(x) = 0
steps.bodyLatex: Set \(f'(x)=0\): \(e^{-x}(\cos x - \sin x) = 0\). Since \(e^{-x} > 0\) for all \(x\), we need \(\cos x - \sin x = 0\), or \(\cos x = \sin x\). This gives \(\tan x = 1\), so \(x = \pi/4 + n\pi\) for integer \(n\).

steps.title: Find the coordinates in the given interval
steps.bodyLatex: For \(0 \le x \le 2\pi\), the solutions are \(x = \pi/4\) and \(x = 5\pi/4\). Evaluate \(f\) at these points: \(f(\pi/4) = e^{-\pi/4}\sin(\pi/4) = e^{-\pi/4} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \frac{e^{-\pi/4}}{\sqrt{2}}\). Similarly, \(f(5\pi/4) = -\frac{e^{-5\pi/4}}{\sqrt{2}}\).

steps.title: Identify intercepts and sketch
steps.bodyLatex: Find x-intercepts by solving \(f(x) = 0\): \(e^{-x}\sin x = 0\) gives \(\sin x = 0\), so \(x = 0, \pi, 2\pi\). These three zeros, together with the two stationary points at \((\pi/4, e^{-\pi/4}/\sqrt{2})\) and \((5\pi/4, -e^{-5\pi/4}/\sqrt{2})\), are enough to sketch the damped sine curve showing the decay towards zero as \(x\) increases.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: Stationary points: \((\pi/4, e^{-\pi/4}/\sqrt{2})\) and \((5\pi/4, -e^{-5\pi/4}/\sqrt{2})\)). Intercepts at \(x = 0, \pi, 2\pi\).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the negative sign when differentiating \(e^{-x}\): remember that \(d/dx(e^{-x}) = -e^{-x}\), not \(e^{-x}\).
Only finding one solution to \(\cos x = \sin x\) instead of both \(\pi/4\) and \(5\pi/4\) in the interval \([0, 2\pi]\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that the y-values are positive for the first stationary point and negative for the second (since \(\sin x\) changes sign at \(x = \pi\)), and that the magnitude decreases (since the exponential factor \(e^{-x}\) decays).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question involves checking independence of Friday and Saturday availability using conditional probability, then finding the marginal probability P(S), and finally applying the complement rule for at least one student being unavailable.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that for part (a), independence requires P(F|S) = P(F). For part (b), use the relationship between conditional probabilities to find P(S). For part (c), use the complement rule with independence across the four students.

steps.title: (a) Check independence of F and S
steps.bodyLatex: Two events F (Friday) and S (Saturday) are independent if \(P(F\mid S)=P(F)\). Here \(P(F\mid S)=\frac18\) but \(P(F)=\frac{3}{10}=\frac{3}{10}\). Since \(\frac18\ne\frac{3{10}\), the events are NOT independent. Alternatively, you could compute \(P(F\cap S)=P(S\mid F)P(F)=\frac13\times\frac{3}{10}=\frac{1}{10}\) and check if this equals \(P(F)P(S)\); since we don't yet know P(S), the direct comparison is clearer.

steps.title: (b) Find P(S) using the conditional probability formula
steps.bodyLatex: We know \(P(F\cap S)=P(S\mid F)P(F)=\frac13\times\frac{3}{10}=\frac{1}{10}\). Using the definition of conditional probability: \[P(F\mid S)=\frac{P(F\cap S)}{P(S)}\] Substituting \(P(F\mid S)=\frac18\) and \(P(F\cap S)=\frac{1}{10}\): \[\frac18=\frac{1/10}{P(S)}\] Solving: \(P(S)=\frac{1/10}{1/8}=\frac{8}{10}=\frac45\). This matches the required result.

steps.title: (c) Find probability at least one student unavailable on Saturday
steps.bodyLatex: Since each student has the same probability of being available on Saturday (\P(S)=\frac45\)), and assuming independence across students (which is reasonable here—their availabilities don't affect each other), the probability all four are available is \[\left(\frac45\right)^4=\frac{256}{625}\] Using the complement rule, the probability that at least one is NOT available is \[1-\left(\frac45\right)^4=1-\frac{256}{625}=\frac{625-256}{625}=\frac{369}{625}\]

commonMistakesLatex: For part (a), students might incorrectly conclude independence without comparing P(F|S) to P(F), or might forget that independence requires equality in BOTH directions (P(F|S) = P(F) AND P(S|F) = P(S)).
For part (c), some students might try to add probabilities of each individual being unavailable rather than using the complement rule with independence, which is much more complicated.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that P(S) = 4/5 makes sense: since P(F|S) < P(F), Saturday availability makes Friday availability less likely, which is consistent with dependence. Also check the final probability: 369/625 ≈ 0.59, meaning there's about a 59% chance at least one student is unavailable—this seems reasonable given 80% availability each.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Find the area under the difference of two exponential curves using definite integration, and find parameter values for two intersection points by converting to a quadratic in $u = e^{-x}$.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), identify which curve is on top by substituting x = 0 (giving e^(-2(0)) = 1 vs e^(-0) - 1/4 = 3/4). The area is the integral of the upper minus lower curve from x = 0 to x = ln2. For part (b), substitute u = e^(-x) > 0 to convert the intersection equation into a quadratic, then apply conditions for two positive roots.

steps.title: Set up the area integral
steps.bodyLatex: At x = 0, e^{-2x} = 1 and e^{-x} - 1/4 = 3/4, so e^{-2x} is above. The bounded region runs from the y-axis (x = 0) to the intersection at x = ln2. Thus: A = \int_0^{\ln2} (e^{-2x} - (e^{-x} - \frac14)) dx = \int_0^{\ln2} (e^{-2x} - e^{-x} + \frac14) dx.

steps.title: Integrate and evaluate
steps.bodyLatex: The antiderivative is -\frac12 e^{-2x} + e^{-x} + \frac{x}{4}. Evaluating from 0 to \ln2: at \ln2 we get -\frac12(\frac14) + \frac12 + \frac{\ln2}{4} = \frac38 + \frac{\ln2}{4}; at 0 we get -\frac12 + 1 = \frac12. Their difference is (\frac38 + \frac{\ln2}{4}) - \frac12 = \frac14\ln2 - \frac18.

steps.title: Convert to quadratic form
steps.bodyLatex: For intersections: e^{-2x} = e^{-x} + k. Let u = e^{-x} > 0, so e^{-2x} = u^2. Then u^2 = u + k, giving u^2 - u - k = 0. This quadratic has discriminant D = 1 + 4k.

steps.title: Apply conditions for two positive roots
steps.bodyLatex: Two intersections require two positive u-values. Need D > 0 (so k > -\frac14) and both roots positive. The larger root u = (1 + \sqrt{1+4k})/2 is always positive. The smaller root (1 - \sqrt{1+4k})/2 > 0 requires \sqrt{1+4k} < 1, so 1+4k < 1 giving k < 0. Hence -\frac14 < k < 0.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that u = e^{-x} > 0 restricts the domain. The quadratic could have two real roots but only one positive, which would give only one intersection point in the original x-variable.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For part (a), verify the area is positive: \frac14\ln2 \approx 0.3466 and \frac18 = 0.125, so area \approx 0.2216. This is reasonable for a small region near the origin. For part (b), test k = -0.1: u^2 - u + 0.1 = 0 has discriminant 0.6, roots 0.5 \pm 0.387, both positive, giving two intersections.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Identify which graph matches the linear equation \(y=-2x+2\) by examining its gradient and \(y\)-intercept.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For a linear equation in the form \(y=mx+c\), immediately identify the gradient \(m\) and \(y\)-intercept \(c\). Here \(m=-2\) (negative, so the line slopes downward) and \(c=2\) (positive, so the line crosses the \(y\)-axis at \(y=2\)).

steps.title: Identify the gradient
steps.bodyLatex: The coefficient of \(x\) in \(y=-2x+2\) is \(-2\). This is the gradient, which is negative. A line with negative gradient goes downwards as you move from left to right.

steps.title: Identify the \(y\)-intercept
steps.bodyLatex: The constant term is \(2\). This is the \(y\)-intercept, meaning the line crosses the \(y\)-axis at the point \((0,2)\). This is above the origin.

steps.title: Match to the correct option
steps.bodyLatex: Look at each graph option. The correct graph must show a line that slopes downward (negative gradient) AND passes through \((0,2)\) on the \(y\)-axis.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.bodyLatex: Option A is the only graph with both a negative gradient and a positive \(y\)-intercept of 2. The other options either have the wrong gradient direction or the wrong intercept.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the sign of the gradient. Remember: a negative gradient means the line goes down from left to right, not up.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that the line crosses the \(y\)-axis at \(0,2)\) and slopes downward. You could also check the \(x\)-intercept: set \(y=0\) gives \(x=1\), so the line also passes through \((1,0)\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: We determine whether adding a low score of 5 changes the median and/or mean of the dataset.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First find the original median (with 6 values, it's the average of the 3rd and 4th). Then add 5 and find the new median (with 7 values, it's the middle 4th value). Finally calculate both means to see which changes.

steps.title: Find the original median
steps.bodyLatex: The original dataset has 6 numbers: 13, 16, 17, 17, 21, 24. With an even number of values, the median is the average of the 3rd and 4th values. Both the 3rd and 4th values are 17, so the original median is \(17\).

steps.title: Find the new median after adding 5
steps.bodyLatex: Adding 5 gives the ordered dataset: 5, 13, 16, 17, 17, 21, 24. Now there are 7 numbers (odd), so the median is the middle 4th value, which is 17. The median stays the same at \(17\).

steps.title: Calculate the new mean
steps.bodyLatex: New sum: \(108 + 5 = 113\). New mean: \(113 \div 7 \approx 16.14\). The mean changes from 18 to approximately 16.14.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: D. The median stays the same and the mean changes.
commonMistakesLatex: Thinking the median must change whenever data is added. With 6 values the median was the average of two 17s, and with 7 values the median is the middle value which is also 17. The median can stay the same when a value is added on either side of the existing median.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The new data point (5) is below the original median of 17, which explains why the mean decreased while the median remained unchanged.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Using the tangent ratio in the right triangle formed by the tower height and distance from the observation point.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the right triangle with the tower height as the opposite side and AB as the adjacent side to the angle of elevation. The tangent ratio directly relates these sides.

steps.title: Set up the right triangle
steps.bodyLatex: In the right triangle with vertex at A, the tower height \(BT = h\) is the side opposite the angle \(26^\circ\), and the distance \(AB\) from the observation point to the tower base is the adjacent side. This gives us opposite = \(h\) and adjacent = \(AB\).

steps.title: Choose the correct trigonometric ratio
steps.bodyLatex: Since we have the opposite side \(h\) and need the adjacent side \(AB\), use the tangent ratio: \(\tan\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{	ext{adjacent}}\). Here \(\tan26^\circ = \frac{h}{AB}\).

steps.title: Solve for AB
steps.bodyLatex: Rearrange the formula: \(AB = \frac{h}{\tan26^\circ}\). Recall that \(\frac{1}{\tan\theta} = \cot\theta\), so \(AB = h\cot26^\circ\).

steps.title: Match to the given options
steps.bodyLatex: The expression \(h\cot26^\circ\) corresponds to option B. This is the correct length of AB.

commonMistakesLatex: Using sine or cosine instead of tangent. Remember: for an angle of elevation problem where you know the opposite (height) and need the adjacent (distance), tangent is the appropriate ratio.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since \(\cot26^\circ > 1\) (because \(\tan26^\circ < 1\)), the distance AB is greater than the height h, which makes practical sense—the observer would be standing some distance away from the tower.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: For the quadratic f(x)=x²-1, the range starts at the minimum value -1 and extends to infinity.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that x² is always non-negative. This tells us the smallest value f(x) can take, and since quadratics grow without bound, we can determine the full range.

steps.title: Identify the key property of x²
steps.bodyLatex: Since x² ≥ 0 for all real x (it's a square), the smallest x² can be is 0. This happens at x = 0.

steps.title: Find the minimum value of f(x)
steps.bodyLatex: Substituting x = 0 into f(x) = x² - 1 gives f(0) = 0² - 1 = -1. So the minimum value is -1.

steps.title: Determine how f(x) behaves as x grows
steps.bodyLatex: As x → ±∞, the x² term dominates and f(x) = x² - 1 → ∞. There is no upper bound.

steps.title: Write the range in interval notation
steps.bodyLatex: The minimum is -1 (included since x = 0 gives f(0) = -1), and there is no maximum. The range is [-1, ∞).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing domain with range: some students might write (-∞, ∞) thinking of x-values rather than y-values. Remember range means output values (y), domain means input values (x).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test the minimum: at x = 0, f(0) = -1, which matches the lower bound of the range. Any other x gives f(x) > -1.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Apply the quotient rule to differentiate \(h(x)=\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}\), then substitute the given values at \(x=1\) to find the gradient.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that \(h(x)\) is a quotient of two functions, so the quotient rule is required. Identify what values are given at \(x=1\) before substituting into the derivative formula.

steps.title: Apply the quotient rule
steps.bodyLatex: For \(h(x)=\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}\), the quotient rule gives \(h'(x)=\frac{f'(x)g(x)-f(x)g'(x)}{[g(x)]^2}\). This is the standard formula to differentiate a ratio of two functions.

steps.title: Substitute the known values at x=1
Complete the denominator
steps.bodyLatex: The denominator is \([g(1)]^2 = 8^2 = 64\). So \(h'(1)=\frac{8}{64}\). This gives the gradient of the tangent at \(x=1\).

steps.title: Simplify the fraction
steps.bodyLatex: Simplify \(\frac{8}{64}=\frac{1}{8}\). This is the gradient of the tangent to \(y=h(x)\) at \(x=1\). The correct option is D.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to square the denominator in the quotient rule, using \(g(1)\) instead of \([g(1)]^2\). Always write \([g(x)]^2\) in the denominator.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify: numerator \(32-24=8\) is positive, denominator \(64\) is positive, so the gradient must be positive. The answer \(1/8\) is positive, which is reasonable.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Find the indefinite integral of (2x+1)^(-2) using u-substitution.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice the inner function 2x+1 has derivative 2. Use u-substitution to simplify the power of -2 integral.

steps.title: Set up substitution
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(u = 2x+1\). Then \(du = 2\,dx\) or \(dx = \frac{1}{2}\,du\). This substitution will transform the integral into a simple power of u.

steps.title: Apply substitution
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute into the integral: \(\int (2x+1)^{-2}\,dx = \int u^{-2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\,du = \frac{1}{2}\int u^{-2}\,du\). Factor out the constant \(\frac12\).

steps.title: Integrate in terms of u
steps.bodyLatex: Using the power rule: \(\int u^{-2}\,du = \frac{u^{-1}}{-1} = -u^{-1}\). So \(\frac12\int u^{-2}\,du = \frac12(-u^{-1}) = -\frac{1}{2u}\).

steps.title: Back-substitute
steps.bodyLatex: Replace \(u\) with \(2x+1\): \(-\frac{1}{2(2x+1)} + C\). This matches option B.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the \(\frac12\) from \(dx = \frac{du}{2}\) and integrating as \(-\frac{1}{2x+1}+C\), which is option A. Always include the Jacobian factor when using u-substitution.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Differentiate your answer: \(d/dx[-\frac{1}{2(2x+1)}] = (2x+1)^{-2}\) ✓.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: The mode of a probability density function is the x-value where f(x) reaches its maximum.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the highest point on the graph. The x-coordinate of this peak gives the mode directly.

steps.title: Recall the definition of mode
steps.bodyLatex: For a continuous random variable with pdf \(f(x)\), the mode is the value of \(x\) where \(f(x)\) attains its maximum value. This is the most likely outcome.

steps.title: Locate the peak on the graph
steps.bodyLatex: Examine the graph to find the highest point. The maximum is labelled at the coordinates \(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3}{2\pi}\).

steps.title: Read the x-coordinate
steps.bodyLatex: The x-coordinate of this maximum point is \(x = \frac{\pi}{4}\). This is where the density function is greatest.

steps.title: Select the matching option
steps.bodyLatex: Option C is \(\frac{\pi}{4}\) which matches our answer. The mode is \(\frac{\pi}{4}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the mode with the maximum value of the function: the mode is the x-value (\(\frac{\pi}{4}\)), not the y-value (\(\frac{3}{2\pi}\)).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The mode should be an x-value, and among the options only A, B and C are x-values in the proper range. \(\frac{\pi}{4}\) is approximately 0.79, which is a reasonable location for the peak.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: For an even function, the integral over \([-2,2]\) equals twice the integral over \([0,2]\). The signed area on \([0,2]\) is the positive area (region A) minus the negative area (region B).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that f is even, so the graph is symmetric about the y-axis. This means the integral from -2 to 2 is twice the integral from 0 to 2. The signed area on [0,2] combines the positive region A (above the x-axis) and negative region B (below the x-axis).

steps.title: Use even function property
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(f\) is even, \(f(-x) = f(x)\), the integral over the symmetric interval \([-2,2]\) equals twice the integral over \([0,2]\): \[\int_{-2}^{2}f(x)\,dx = 2\int_{0}^{2}f(x)\,dx\]

steps.title: Find signed area on [0,2]
steps.bodyLatex: Region A lies above the x-axis (positive area = \(\frac12\)), and region B lies below the x-axis (negative area = \(-\frac32\)). The signed integral is the sum: \[\int_{0}^{2}f(x)\,dx = \frac12 + \left(-\frac32\right) = -1\]

steps.title: Double to find total integral
steps.bodyLatex: Multiply by 2 for the symmetric interval: \[\int_{-2}^{2}f(x)\,dx = 2 \times (-1) = -2\]

steps.title: Select matching option
steps.bodyLatex: The value -2 corresponds to option C.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to subtract the negative region B's area. Some students add both areas instead of treating region B as negative when calculating the signed integral.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since region B (negative area \(3/2\)) is larger than region A (positive area \(1/2\)), the integral over any interval containing both regions should be negative, which our answer -2 confirms.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Using the complement rule for at least one win with independent games, solve for the single-game win probability p, then calculate p² for winning both games.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that the games are independent and Liam is 'equally likely to win each game', meaning both games have the same win probability p. Use the complement rule: P(at least one win) = 1 - P(lose both).

steps.title: Define the single-game win probability
steps.bodyLatex: Let p be the probability Liam wins any single game. Since the games are independent, the probability he loses a particular game is (1-p). The probability he loses both games is (1-p)².

steps.title: Apply the complement rule
steps.bodyLatex: P(at least one win) = 1 - P(lose both) = 1 - (1-p)². We know this equals 0.8, so: 1 - (1-p)² = 0.8. Rearranging gives (1-p)² = 0.2.

steps.title: Solve for p
steps.bodyLatex: Taking square roots: 1-p = √0.2 ≈ 0.4472. Therefore p = 1 - √0.2 ≈ 0.5528. This is the probability Liam wins one game.

steps.title: Find probability of winning both
steps.bodyLatex: Since the games are independent, P(both wins) = p × p = p². Substituting: p² = (1 - √0.2)² ≈ (0.5528)² ≈ 0.306, which is approximately 31%.

commonMistakesLatex: Incorrectly assuming the probability of winning both games is simply half of 80%, giving 40%. This ignores the exponential relationship between the probabilities when calculating complements.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify: If p ≈ 0.55, then P(at least one win) = 1 - (0.45)² = 1 - 0.2025 ≈ 0.7975 ≈ 80%, which checks out.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Determine the composite function g(f(x)) by considering what values f(x) produces as input to g.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First identify the range of f(x) — since f(x) ≥ 0 always, the composition g(f(x)) only uses the non-negative input portion of g. Look for the option graph that matches this restricted input behavior.

steps.title: Find the range of f(x)
steps.bodyLatex: From the given graph of y = f(x), observe that f(x) ≥ 0 for all x in its domain. The minimum occurs at x = 0 where f(0) = 0. This means f maps every x to a non-negative output.

steps.title: Determine the effective input to g
steps.bodyLatex: When computing g(f(x)), the input to g is always f(x) which is non-negative. So we only need the part of g's graph where the input (the x-axis value for g) is ≥ 0.

steps.title: Match to the correct option
steps.bodyLatex: Option B shows the resulting graph behaviour — it reflects that we're composing with the non-negative portion of g's input pattern, consistent with f(x) ≥ 0.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.bodyLatex: The graph in option B correctly represents y = g(f(x)) because it reflects the even input pattern from f being passed to g's restricted domain.

commonMistakesLatex: Treating g(f(x)) as using all of g's graph — remember f(x) ≥ 0 limits the input to g to only non-negative values.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify: if f(x) always produces non-negative outputs, then g(f(x)) should only use the right half of g's graph (where input ≥ 0).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question involves reading and interpreting a cumulative frequency table and using a Pareto chart to identify which complaint categories account for 80% of total complaints.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), identify which rows contribute to each cumulative value by adding frequencies sequentially. For part (b), find where the cumulative percentage first reaches or exceeds 80% on the Pareto chart.

steps.title: Find value A in cumulative frequency
steps.bodyLatex: The cumulative frequency column adds each complaint type's frequency progressively. Since the table shows A appears after the first two rows, we add those frequencies: \(A = 98 + 62 = 160\). This represents all complaints up to (but not including) the damaged items row.

steps.title: Find value B in cumulative percentage
steps.bodyLatex: The damaged items row has a cumulative frequency of 192 (160 + 32). With 200 total complaints, the cumulative percentage is \(B = \frac{192}{200} \times 100 = 96\). This is the percentage of all complaints accounted for up to and including damaged items.

steps.title: Read the Pareto chart for 80% threshold
steps.bodyLatex: A Pareto chart displays complaint types in descending order by frequency (left axis) with a cumulative percentage line (right axis). The manager wants to address complaints representing 80% or more cumulatively. Find where the cumulative percentage line first reaches 80%.

steps.title: Identify the complaints to address
steps.bodyLatex: On the Pareto chart, the cumulative percentage reaches 80% after the "stock shortage" category. Adding "delivery fee" (which comes before stock shortage in the cumulative count) gives exactly 80%. These two categories are: stock shortage and delivery fee complaints.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) \(A = 160\), \(B = 96\). (b) Stock shortage and delivery fee complaints.
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing which rows add to A—make sure you add the frequencies in order from the top of the table until just before the row containing A.
Forgetting that the cumulative percentage line on a Pareto chart relates to the right-hand axis, not the left.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify: 98 + 62 = 160 (checks out for A). Also verify that 160 + 32 = 192, giving 192/200 × 100 = 96% (checks out for B).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question involves inverse variation, where melting time $M$ varies inversely with temperature $T$, giving $M = \frac{k}{T}$. Using the given point $(15, 12)$ to find $k = 180$, then calculating $M$ for other temperatures.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice first that 'varies inversely' means $M = \frac{k}{T}$ (not $kT$). The constant $k$ is found by substituting the known values $T = 15$ and $M = 12$. This type of relationship produces a rectangular hyperbola when graphed.

steps.title: Set up inverse variation equation
steps.bodyLatex: Since $M$ varies inversely with $T$, the relationship is $M = \frac{k}{T}$, where $k$ is the constant of variation. This is different from direct variation ($M = kT$) and you must use the reciprocal form.

steps.title: Find the constant $k$
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the known values: when $T = 15$ and $M = 12$, we get $12 = \frac{k}{15}$. Solving gives $k = 12 \times 15 = 180$.

steps.title: Write the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Replace $k$ in the formula: $M = \frac{180}{T}$. This is the equation relating melting time to temperature. You can verify it: $M(15) = \frac{180}{15} = 12$ ✓

steps.title: Calculate table values
steps.bodyLatex: Using $M = \frac{180}{T}$: at $T = 5$, $M = \frac{180}{5} = 36$; at $T = 15$, $M = 12$ (given); at $T = 30$, $M = \frac{180}{30} = 6$. These three points show the inverse relationship.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) $M = \frac{180}{T}$. (b) Table values: $T = 5 \rightarrow M = 36$, $T = 15 \rightarrow M = 12$, $T = 30 \rightarrow M = 6$. The graph shows a rectangular hyperbola decreasing from left to right.
commonMistakesLatex: Using direct variation $M = kT$ instead of inverse variation $M = \frac{k}{T}$. Remember: 'varies inversely' means as one increases, the other decreases, so use division not multiplication.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check: as temperature doubles from 15 to 30, time halves from 12 to 6 — this confirms the inverse relationship is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Apply the trapezoidal rule with two equal subintervals (n=2) to approximate the definite integral.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice the interval [0,2] is divided into two equal subintervals of width h=1. Evaluate the function f(x)=√(1+x²) at the three points x=0,1,2, then apply the trapezoidal rule formula.

steps.title: Calculate the step size
steps.bodyLatex: With two applications of the trapezoidal rule, we divide [0,2] into n=2 equal subintervals. The width of each subinterval is h = (b-a)/n = (2-0)/2 = 1.

steps.title: Evaluate the function at grid points
Apply the trapezoidal rule formula
steps.bodyLatex:
For n=2 subintervals, the trapezoidal rule is:
\[T =  rac{h}{2}\left[f(x_0) + 2f(x_1) + f(x_2)ight]\]
Substituting h=1 and the function values:
\[T =  rac{1}{2}\left[1 + 2\sqrt2 + \sqrt5ight]\]
\[T \approx  rac{1}{2}(1 + 2.8284 + 2.2361) =  rac{1}{2}(6.0645) = 3.0322\]
Rounding to 2 decimal places gives 3.03.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the factor of 2 on f(1). With n=2 subintervals, the middle point x=1 is used twice in the trapezoidal rule, hence the term 2f(1).
Using the wrong formula—the correct formula is (h/2)[f₀ + 2f₁ + f₂], not h[f₀ + f₁ + f₂].
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The actual value of ∫₀²√(1+x²)dx is approximately 3.17, so 3.03 is a reasonable underestimate (the trapezoidal rule consistently underestimates when the function is convex, as √(1+x²) is on [0,2]).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Given a graph of y = k sin(ax), identify k from the amplitude and a from the period.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First identify the amplitude from the vertical distance between the maximum and midline. Then use the period formula for sin(ax), which is 2π/a, to find a.

steps.title: Find k from amplitude
steps.bodyLatex: The amplitude is the vertical distance from the midline to a maximum (or minimum). From the graph, this distance is 4. Since the amplitude of y = k sin(ax) equals |k|, we have k = 4 (taking the positive value for amplitude).

steps.title: Find the period from the graph
steps.bodyLatex: The period is the horizontal distance for one complete cycle—from one peak to the next peak. The graph shows a full cycle spans 6π units, so the period is 6π.

steps.title: Use the period formula to find a
steps.bodyLatex: For y = k sin(ax), the period is given by 2π/a. Setting this equal to the observed period: 2π/a = 6π. Solving: 2π = 6πa, so a = 2π ÷ 6π = 1/3.

steps.title: State the values
steps.bodyLatex: k = 4 (amplitude) and a = 1/3 (horizontal stretch factor). This gives the equation y = 4 sin(⅓x).

commonMistakesLatex: Taking the period as the distance from zero to zero instead of peak to peak. Make sure you measure one complete cycle.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify: period = 2π ÷ (1/3) = 6π ✓ Matches the graph.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question uses the law of total probability to find P(5), then applies Bayes' theorem to find the conditional probability P(special | 5).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice there are two types of dice with different probabilities of rolling 5. First find the overall P(5) by considering both selection probability and rolling probability, then use this to find the conditional probability.

steps.title: Identify probabilities for each die type
steps.bodyLatex: Two normal dice: selecting one gives $\frac23$ (since $2$ out of $3$ dice are normal). Each normal die has $P(5) = \frac16$ (one face is 5). The special die: selecting it gives $\frac13$. The special die has $P(5) = \frac36 = \frac12$ (three faces are 5).

steps.title: Apply law of total probability for P(5)
steps.bodyLatex: $P(5) = P(\text{normal}) \times P(5|\text{normal}) + P(\text{special}) \times P(5|\text{special}) = \frac23 \times \frac16 + \frac13 \times \frac36 = \frac{2}{18} + \frac{3}{18} = \frac{5}{18}$.

steps.title: Find joint probability P(special AND 5)
steps.bodyLatex: Use multiplication rule: $P(\text{special} \cap 5) = P(\text{special}) \times P(5|\text{special}) = \frac13 \times \frac36 = \frac{1}{6}$.

steps.title: Apply definition of conditional probability
steps.bodyLatex: $P(\text{special}|5) = \frac{P(\text{special} \cap 5)}{P(5)} = \frac{1/6}{5/18} = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{18}{5} = \frac{3}{5}$.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to multiply by the selection probability of each die type — you must account for whether the selected die is normal or special before calculating the rolling probability.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that P(special | 5) is greater than P(special), which is $\frac13$. Since $\frac35 > \frac13$, this makes sense: seeing a 5 increases the likelihood the die is special.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Find the area between the line y=2x+3 and the parabola y=x² between their intersection points at x=-1 and x=3.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First identify which curve is above by substituting a test point (like x=0): the line gives y=3 while the parabola gives y=0, so the line is above. The enclosed area is the definite integral of (line minus parabola) from x=-1 to x=3.

steps.title: Identify the region
steps.bodyLatex: The intersection points are given as x=-1 and x=3. To determine which curve forms the upper boundary, substitute x=0 into both equations: the line gives y=2(0)+3=3 and the parabola gives y=0²=0. Since 3>0, the line lies above the parabola over the enclosed region.

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.bodyLatex: The vertical distance between the curves is (2x+3)−x². The enclosed area is the definite integral of this difference from x=-1 to x=3: A=∫_{-1}^{3}(2x+3−x²)dx.

steps.title: Integrate
steps.bodyLatex: Find the antiderivative: ∫(2x+3−x²)dx = x²+3x−x³/3 + C. Evaluate from -1 to 3: [3²+3(3)−3³/3]−[(-1)²+3(-1)−(-1)³/3] = (9+9−9)−(1−3+1/3).

commonMistakesLatex: Incorrectly setting up (parabola minus line), which gives a negative area. Always subtract the lower function from the upper function, or take the absolute value at the end.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The region is roughly 4 units wide with average height around 2-3 units, giving area roughly 10-12 square units. The answer 32/3≈10.67 fits this estimate.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: The card counts form an arithmetic sequence with first term 3 and common difference 3. We use the sum formula for an arithmetic series to find the total cards and solve for the number of rows.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that each row requires 3 more cards than the previous row—this is an arithmetic sequence with a = 3 and d = 3. The sum of an arithmetic series formula S_n = n/2(2a + (n-1)d) lets us calculate totals or solve for n.

steps.title: Identify the arithmetic sequence
steps.bodyLatex: The rows require 3, 6, 9, ... cards. This is an arithmetic sequence with first term a = 3 and common difference d = 3. The nth row requires 3 + (n-1)×3 = 3n cards.

steps.title: Set up equation for part (b)
steps.bodyLatex: We have S_n = 828. Using the formula: \frac{n}{2}(2×3 + (n-1)×3) = 828. Simplify: \frac{n}{2}(6 + 3n - 3) = \frac{n}{2}(3n + 3) = 828.

steps.title: Solve for n
steps.bodyLatex: Rejecting the negative root, we get n = 23 rows.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to use the positive solution when solving the quadratic equation—n must be positive as it represents a number of rows.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Use the chain rule to differentiate (x² + 1)⁴, then recognise that x(x² + 1)³ is 1/8 of the derivative found in part (a) to integrate by reversal.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), identify the outer function u⁴ where u = x² + 1, then apply the chain rule. For part (b), notice the integrand x(x² + 1)³ matches the structure from part (a) — use substitution or recognise it as 1/8 of the derivative.

steps.title: Apply the chain rule to part (a)
steps.bodyLatex: Let u = x² + 1, so y = u⁴. By the chain rule: \(\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy}{du}\cdot\frac{du}{dx}=4u³\cdot2x=8x(x²+1)³\). The power rule on the outer function gives 4u³, and differentiating the inner function gives 2x.

steps.title: Set up substitution for the integral
steps.bodyLatex: In \(\int x(x²+1)³\,dx\), let u = x² + 1. Then du = 2x dx, so x dx = \(\frac{1}{2}\)du. The integral becomes \(∫ u³·\frac{1}{2}\,du = \frac12∫ u³\,du\).

steps.title: Integrate with respect to u
steps.bodyLatex: \(∫ u³\,du = \frac{u⁴}{4}\). Multiplying by \(\frac12\) gives \(\frac{1}{2}·\frac{u⁴}{4}=\frac{u⁴}{8}\). Replace u with (x² + 1) to get \(∫ x(x²+1)³\,dx=\frac{(x²+1)⁴}{8}+C\).

steps.title: Verify using the 'hence' connection
steps.bodyLatex: From part (a), \(\frac{dy}{dx}=8x(x²+1)³\). Rearranging: \(x(x²+1)³\,dx=\frac18\,dy\). Integrating gives \(\int x(x²+1)³\,dx=\frac18y+C=\frac18(x²+1)⁴+C\), matching the result above.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the factor of 2 from differentiating x² inside the chain rule, giving 4x instead of 8x. Double-check: d/dx(x²) = 2x, so multiply by this factor.
Omitting the constant of integration C in the indefinite integral. Always include +C when the integral is indefinite.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Differentiate \((\frac18(x²+1)⁴\): \(8x(x²+1)³\) × 1/8 = x(x²+1)³, confirming the integral is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question requires expressing a transformed quadratic in vertex form to identify the translation distance $m$ and dilation scale factor $k$.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that the three transformations (right shift, vertical stretch, down shift) combine to give $g(x)=k(x-m)^2-5$. Comparing this form with the given $g(x)=3x^2-12x+7$ by completing the square reveals $m$ and $k$.

steps.title: Write the general transformed form
steps.bodyLatex: Starting with $f(x)=x^2$, apply the three transformations in order: translate $m$ units right gives $(x-m)^2$, dilate vertically by $k$ gives $k(x-m)^2$, then translate $5$ units down gives $k(x-m)^2-5$. Thus $g(x)=k(x-m)^2-5$.

steps.title: Complete the square for the given quadratic
steps.bodyLatex: Given $g(x)=3x^2-12x+7$, factor out the coefficient of $x^2$ from the first two terms: $3(x^2-4x)+7$. Now complete the square inside the brackets by adding and subtracting $4$ (since $(\frac{4}{2})^2=4$).

steps.title: Express in vertex form
steps.bodyLatex: $3(x^2-4x+4-4)+7=3(x-2)^2-12+7=3(x-2)^2-5$. This is now in the form $k(x-m)^2-5$, where we can directly read off the parameters.

steps.title: Identify m and k
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing $3(x-2)^2-5$ with $k(x-m)^2-5$, we see $k=3$ and $m=2$. The vertical dilation is by factor 3, and the horizontal translation is 2 units right.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to factor out the leading coefficient before completing the square, which gives the wrong vertex form. Always factor out the coefficient of $x^2$ first.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Substitute back: $3(x-2)^2-5=3x^2-12x+12-5=3x^2-12x+7$, which matches the given function.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests evaluation of an exponential model and differentiation to find instantaneous rates of change.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), substitute t = 0. For (b), substitute t = 24 and evaluate. For (c), differentiate the exponential function, then substitute t = 24.

steps.title: Find initial population
steps.bodyLatex:
The initial number occurs at the start of the experiment, so set \(t = 0\).
\[N(0) = 200e^{0.013 \times 0} = 200e^0 = 200 \times 1 = 200\]
There were initially 200 bacteria.

steps.title: Evaluate at 24 hours
steps.bodyLatex:
Substitute \(t = 24\) into the model:
\[N(24) = 200e^{0.013 \times 24} = 200e^{0.312}\]
Using \(e^{0.312} \approx 1.3663\), we get \(N(24) \approx 200 \times 1.3663 \approx 273.26\), so approximately 273 bacteria.

steps.title: Differentiate the model
steps.bodyLatex:
The derivative of \(N(t) = 200e^{0.013t}\) uses \(\frac{d}{dt}(e^{kt}) = ke^{kt}\):
\[\frac{dN}{dt} = 200 \times 0.013 \times e^{0.013t} = 0.013 \times 200e^{0.013t} = 0.013N(t)\]
This gives the instantaneous rate of increase in bacteria per hour.

steps.title: Evaluate the rate at t = 24
steps.bodyLatex:
Substitute the value from step 2 into the derivative:
\[\frac{dN}{dt}\Big|_{t=24} = 0.013 \times N(24) \approx 0.013 \times 273.26 \approx 3.55\]
The bacteria are increasing at approximately 3.55 per hour at 24 hours.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) 200 bacteria (b) approximately 273 bacteria (c) approximately 3.55 bacteria per hour
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to evaluate the exponent correctly when finding N(24); use a calculator or remember that e^0.312 ≈ 1.366.
Confusing the rate dN/dt with the population value N(t); the rate is the derivative, not the function value.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The rate constant 0.013 is the continuous growth rate, so at 24 hours, 0.013 × 273 ≈ 3.55 is reasonable since about 1.3% of 273 is 3.55.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Comparing future values of a lump sum investment (Option 1) versus a quarterly annuity (Option 2) over 10 years, using compound interest formulas and annuity tables.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For Option 1, identify it as a lump sum growing with monthly compounding - use FV = P(1+i)^n. For Option 2, identify quarterly deposits as an annuity - read the table factor for n=40 periods at i=0.006. The key is correctly matching the compounding periods to the table.

steps.title: Calculate Option 1 future value
steps.bodyLatex: Option 1 is a single lump sum of \$40,000 earning 1.2% p.a. compounded monthly. The monthly interest rate is \(i = \frac{0.012}{12} = 0.001\). Over 10 years there are \(n = 10 \times 12 = 120\) compounding periods. Using \(FV = P(1+i)^n\): \(FV_1 = 40000(1.001)^{120} \approx \$45,097.17\).

steps.title: Set up Option 2 parameters
steps.bodyLatex: Option 2 involves \$1,000 deposited at the end of each quarter (an ordinary annuity). The annual rate is 2.4% compounded quarterly, so the quarterly rate is \(i = \frac{0.024}{4} = 0.006\). Over 10 years there are \(n = 10 \times 4 = 40\) quarters. These values match the table format.

steps.title: Read the annuity table factor
steps.bodyLatex: Find the future value interest factor for an annuity of \$1 in the table for \(n=40\) periods at \(i=0.6\%\) (or 0.006). The table gives the factor as 45.05630. This factor represents the sum of all \(1+i\) terms accumulated over the 40 periods.

steps.title: Calculate difference between options
steps.bodyLatex: Option 2 future value: \(FV_2 = 1000 \times 45.05630 = \$45,056.30\). The difference is \(FV_1 - FV_2 = 45097.17 - 45056.30 = \$40.87\). Option 1 (lump sum) slightly outperforms Option 2 (quarterly deposits) by \$40.87 after 10 years.

commonMistakesLatex: Using the wrong number of periods in the table - remember 10 years of quarterly deposits means 40 periods, not 10. Also confusing the annual and quarterly rates when reading the table.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Both results are around \$45,000 which is reasonable since \$40,000 growing at 1.2% for 10 years would be approximately \$40,000 \times 1.127 \approx \$45,080, and \$1,000 \times 40 \approx \$40,000 plus interest would reach a similar range.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: For a cubic on a closed interval, find stationary points by differentiating, then evaluate the function at all critical points and endpoints to determine global extrema.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First differentiate to find stationary points. Check whether each stationary point lies within the given domain, then evaluate the original function at all candidates (stationary points plus endpoints).

steps.title: Differentiate the function
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(y=x^3-6x^2+8\), differentiate: \(\frac{dy}{dx}=3x^2-12x=3x(x-4)\). This is a quadratic that equals zero at stationary points.

steps.title: Find stationary points
steps.bodyLatex: Set \(\frac{dy}{dx}=0\): \(3x(x-4)=0\) gives \(x=0\) or \(x=4\). Both lie in the domain \([-1,7]\), so both must be checked as candidates for extrema.

steps.title: Evaluate at all candidates
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate \(y\) at stationary points and endpoints: \(y(-1)=1\), \(y(0)=8\), \(y(4)=-24\), \(y(7)=57\). List these values to compare.

steps.title: Identify global extrema
steps.bodyLatex: The smallest value is \(-24\) (at \(x=4\)), giving the global minimum. The largest value is \(57\) (at \(x=7\)), giving the global maximum.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: Global minimum: \(-24\) at \(x=4\). Global maximum: \(57\) at \(x=7\).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to check whether stationary points lie within the given domain — always verify \(x\) values are in the interval before evaluating.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify each calculation by substituting back into the original \(y=x^3-6x^2+8\): \(4^3-6(4)^2+8=64-96+8=-24\) and \(7^3-6(7)^2+8=343-294+8=57\) ✓

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: A cosine model d = 1.3 - 0.6cos(4πt/25) describes tidal depth. Finding extremes uses cos range [-1,1], period uses coefficient of t, and the ≥1 metre condition solves a cosine equation.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice this is a cosine function shifted vertically. The amplitude 0.6 and base 1.3 directly give min and max. The period comes from the coefficient of t inside cosine. For part (c), solve d = 1 to find boundary times, then find the interval between them.

steps.title: Find minimum and maximum depth
steps.bodyLatex: The cosine function ranges from -1 to 1. At low tide, cos(4πt/25) = 1 giving d_min = 1.3 - 0.6(1) = 0.7 m. At high tide, cos(4πt/25) = -1 giving d_max = 1.3 - 0.6(-1) = 1.9 m.

steps.title: Find the period between low tides
steps.bodyLatex: For cos(kt), the period is 2π/k. Here k = 4π/25, so period T = 2π ÷ (4π/25) = 2π × (25/4π) = 25/2 = 12.5 hours. This is the time from one low tide to the next.

steps.title: Solve for when depth equals 1 metre
steps.bodyLatex: Set d = 1: 1 = 1.3 - 0.6cos(4πt/25). Then 0.6cos(4πt/25) = 0.3, so cos(4πt/25) = 0.5 = cos(π/3). This gives 4πt/25 = π/3 or 5π/3 (within one period).

steps.title: Find duration above 1 metre
steps.bodyLatex: Solving 4πt/25 = π/3 gives t = 25/12 hours. Solving 4πt/25 = 5π/3 gives t = 125/12 hours. The interval is 125/12 - 25/12 = 100/12 = 25/3 = 8⅓ hours.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) Low tide: 0.7 m, High tide: 1.9 m. (b) 12.5 hours. (c) 25/3 hours (or 8⅓ hours).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the period is 2π divided by the coefficient of t, not the reciprocal. Some students use period = 2π/(4π/25) = 12.5 hours correctly, but may confuse it with the angular frequency.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: At t = 0 (low tide), d = 1.3 - 0.6(1) = 0.7 m. At t = 6.25 (quarter period = half the time to high tide), d = 1.3 - 0.6(0) = 1.3 m. At t = 12.5 (next low tide), d = 1.3 - 0.6(1) = 0.7 m. The model is consistent.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests interpretation of bivariate data output including correlation coefficient, regression line equation, and scatterplot pattern in context.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice the key numbers: the positive correlation coefficient (0.4564) tells us the direction and strength, while the slope (1.85) from the regression equation tells us the rate of change. The scatterplot pattern provides additional visual insight.

steps.title: Identify the relationship direction
steps.bodyLatex: Both the correlation coefficient (r = 0.4564, positive) and the slope of the line of best fit (1.85, positive) indicate a positive association. As character age increases, actor age tends to increase. The relationship appears roughly linear on the scatterplot.

steps.title: Assess the strength of association
steps.bodyLatex: The correlation coefficient r = 0.4564 falls in the range 0.3 to 0.7, indicating a moderate positive correlation. This is not a strong relationship—there's considerable scatter about the line. A value closer to 1 would indicate stronger linear association.

steps.title: Interpret the regression slope
steps.bodyLatex: The line of best fit is y = -7.51 + 1.85x, where x is character age and y is predicted actor age. The slope of 1.85 means for each additional year of character age, the predicted actor age increases by approximately 1.85 years.

steps.title: Interpret contextually
steps.bodyLatex: The scatterplot shows actors playing teenage characters range roughly from 14 to 30 years old—actors need not be teenagers themselves. The widest spread of actor ages occurs around character age 15, indicating characters aged about 15 are played by actors of widely varying ages.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The data show a positive, approximately linear relationship with moderate strength (r = 0.4564). The slope of 1.85 indicates that for each additional year of character age, predicted actor age increases by about 1.85 years. Teenage characters are played by actors aged roughly 14–30, so actors are often older than the characters they portray.
commonMistakesLatex: Ignoring the intercept value (-7.51) — while mathematically part of the equation, it has no practical meaning since characters cannot be aged 0.
Confusing which variable is x and which is y — here x is character age, y is actor age, so the slope shows how actor age changes with character age.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check: Does the sign of the correlation match the sign of the slope? Yes — both are positive, confirming a positive relationship.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Find $x$ where both the first and second derivatives of $f(x)=\sin(2x)$ equal specified values, by solving each derivative equation and finding their intersection.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice we have two conditions to satisfy simultaneously. Compute both derivatives first, then solve each equation independently for $2x$, and identify the common solution that satisfies both.

steps.title: Compute the derivatives
steps.bodyLatex: Using the chain rule: $f'(x)=2\cos(2x)$. Differentiating again: $f''(x)=\frac{d}{dx}[2\cos(2x)]=-4\sin(2x)$. These are the two expressions we need to set equal to the given values.

steps.title: Solve the first derivative condition
steps.bodyLatex: Set $2\cos(2x)=-\sqrt3$, so $\cos(2x)=-\sqrt3/2$. The angles in $[0,2\pi)$ with cosine $-\sqrt3/2$ are $2x=5\pi/6$ and $2x=7\pi/6$. This gives $x=5\pi/12$ or $7\pi/12$ in the required range.

steps.title: Solve the second derivative condition
steps.bodyLatex: Set $-4\sin(2x)=2$, so $\sin(2x)=-1/2$. The angles in $[0,2\pi)$ with sine $-1/2$ are $2x=7\pi/6$ and $2x=11\pi/6$. This gives $x=7\pi/12$ or $11\pi/12$ in the required range.

steps.title: Find the common solution
steps.bodyLatex: From Step 2: $2x=5\pi/6$ or $7\pi/6$. From Step 3: $2x=7\pi/6$ or $11\pi/6$. The only common value is $2x=7\pi/6$. Hence $x=7\pi/12$, which lies in $(0,\pi)$.

commonMistakesLatex: Selecting $x=5\pi/12$ from the first condition without checking it also satisfies the second derivative equation. Always verify both conditions simultaneously.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Use the given 60% information to establish the position of 860 relative to the mean, then apply normal distribution properties (symmetry and the 68% within one standard deviation) to find the percentage between 820 and 920 hours.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that 860 is only 20 hours above the mean of 840—this is 0.25 standard deviations. The key is using the given 60% to deduce what percentage lies in each segment, then combining the appropriate segments to get the answer.

steps.title: Establish the position of 860
steps.bodyLatex: Since 860 hours is 20 hours above the mean of 840, this is $\frac{20}{80}=0.25$ standard deviations. The given information tells us 60% are below this value. The standard normal tells us that 60% corresponds to approximately $z=0.25$, confirming our model is consistent.

steps.title: Find percentage between mean and 860
steps.bodyLatex: Since 50% of any normal distribution lies below the mean, the remaining 10% (60% − 50%) must lie between the mean (840) and 860 hours. So about 10% of batteries last between 840 and 860 hours.

steps.title: Use symmetry for 820 to 840
steps.bodyLatex: Since 820 hours is the same distance (20 hours = 0.25σ) below the mean as 860 is above, by normal distribution symmetry, approximately 10% of batteries also lie between 820 and 840 hours.

steps.title: Apply the 68% rule and combine
steps.bodyLatex: The empirical rule states 68% of values lie within one standard deviation (760 to 920). By symmetry, half of this—34%—lies between the mean (840) and 920. Now add the two segments: 10% (820 to 840) + 34% (840 to 920) = 44%.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: Approximately 44\% of the batteries have a lifespan between 820 and 920 hours.
commonMistakesLatex: Using the raw distance (80 hours) instead of standard deviations when comparing to the mean, or forgetting to halve the 68% when using the empirical rule to find just one side of the distribution.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Differentiating $f(x)=xe^{-2x}$ twice to find stationary points and points of inflection, then using second derivative tests to classify the stationary point and sketch the curve.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that $f'(x)$ is already given in a factored form $e^{-2x}(1-2x)$. This makes finding $f''(x)$ straightforward using product rule. Stationary points occur where $f'(x)=0$, and inflection points where $f''(x)=0$. Use $f''$ to classify the stationary point.

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.bodyLatex:
Given $f'(x)=e^{-2x}-2xe^{-2x}=e^{-2x}(1-2x)$. Differentiate using the product rule:
\[f''(x)=\frac{d}{dx}\big(e^{-2x}\big)(1-2x)+e^{-2x}\cdot\frac{d}{dx}(1-2x)\]
\[=(-2)e^{-2x}(1-2x)+e^{-2x}(-2)=-2e^{-2x}(1-2x+1)\]
\[=-2e^{-2x}(2-2x)=4(x-1)e^{-2x}\]
This matches the required result.

steps.title: Find the stationary point
steps.bodyLatex:
For a stationary point, set $f'(x)=0$:
\[e^{-2x}(1-2x)=0\]
Since $e^{-2x}>0$ for all $x$, we need $1-2x=0$, giving $x=\frac12$.
Substitute into $f(x)$:
\[f\left(\frac12\right)=\frac12 \cdot e^{-1}=\frac{1}{2e}\]
So the stationary point is $\left(\frac12,\frac{1}{2e}\right)$.

steps.title: Classify the stationary point
steps.bodyLatex:
Evaluate $f''$ at $x=\frac12$:
\[f''\left(\frac12\right)=4\left(\frac12-1\right)e^{-1}=4\left(-\frac12\right)e^{-1}=-2e^{-1}<0\]
Since $f''\left(\frac12\right)<0$, the stationary point is a local maximum. The negative second derivative indicates concave down at this point.

steps.title: Find the inflection point and sketch
steps.bodyLatex:
A point of inflection occurs when $f''(x)=0$ and concavity changes:
\[4(x-1)e^{-2x}=0\Rightarrow x=1\]
At $x=1$: $y=f(1)=1\cdot e^{-2}=e^{-2}$.
The curve passes through $(0,0)$ (since $f(0)=0$), has a local maximum at $\left(\frac12,\frac{1}{2e}\right)$, an inflection point at $(1,e^{-2})$, and approaches $y=0$ as $x\to\infty$ (the $x$-axis is a horizontal asymptote).

steps.title: Check
steps.bodyLatex: Verify the inflection point by testing concavity on either side. For $x<1$, $x-1<0$ so $f''<0$ (concave down). For $x>1$, $x-1>0$ so $f''>0$ (concave up). The sign change confirms an inflection point at $x=1$.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: Stationary point: $\left(\frac12,\frac{1}{2e}\right)$, a local maximum. Point of inflection: $(1,e^{-2})$. The curve passes through $(0,0)$ with $x$-axis as horizontal asymptote as $x\to\infty$.
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that $e^{-2x}>0$ for all $x$, and incorrectly setting $e^{-2x}=0$ when solving $f'(x)=0$. Always note that the exponential factor never equals zero.
Confusing the stationary point with the inflection point. The stationary point is where $f'=0$; the inflection point is where $f''=0$. They are different locations on this curve.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The $y$-value at the maximum is positive ($\frac{1}{2e}\approx0.18$), and the inflection point occurs to the right of the maximum at $x=1$, which makes sense for this type of curve.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Finding the area of a circular segment involves subtracting a right triangle from a sector with angle π/4. The hyperbola parameters are found by solving simultaneous equations from the two given points.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), notice that (1,1) lies on the circle x²+y²=2, giving radius √2 and angle θ = π/4 (since tan θ = 1). For part (b), substitute both points (0,0) and (1,1) into the hyperbola equation to get two equations in a and b. For part (c), integrate the hyperbola from 0 to 1 and add the circular segment area.

steps.title: Find the sector area
steps.bodyLatex: The circle has radius √2 (since (√2)² = 2). The point (1,1) makes angle θ = arctan(1/1) = π/4 with the x-axis. The sector area is (1/2)r²θ = (1/2)(√2)²(π/4) = (1/2)(2)(π/4) = π/4.

steps.title: Subtract the triangle to get segment area
steps.bodyLatex: The chord from O to (1,1) creates a right triangle with the x-axis and the vertical line x=1. This triangle has base 1 and height 1, so area = (1/2)(1)(1) = 1/2. The circular segment area = π/4 − 1/2.

steps.title: Find hyperbola parameters a and b
steps.bodyLatex: Substituting (0,0): 0 = a/b − 1, so a = b. Substituting (1,1): 1 = a/(b−1) − 1. With a = b, this gives 1 = b/(b−1) − 1, so 2 = b/(b−1). Solving: 2(b−1) = b, giving 2b − 2 = b, so b = 2. Hence a = 2.

steps.title: Calculate the shaded region area
steps.bodyLatex: The hyperbola is y = 2/(2−x) − 1. The area under it from 0 to 1 is ∫₀¹(2/(2−x) − 1)dx. Let u = 2−x, then du = −dx: = −2∫₂¹(1/u)du = −2[ln u]₂¹ = −2(ln 1 − ln 2) = 2 ln 2. So area = 2 ln 2 − 1. Adding the circular segment: 2 ln 2 − 1 + π/4 − 1/2 = 2 ln 2 + π/4 − 3/2.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to subtract the triangular area when calculating the circular segment, or making sign errors when integrating the hyperbola (remember the antiderivative of 1/(2−x) involves ln|2−x|).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that (1,1) satisfies both the circle equation (1²+1²=2 ✓) and the hyperbola equation with a=b=2: y = 2/(2−1) − 1 = 2 − 1 = 1 ✓

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: The rectangles under y = 2^(-x) form a geometric series with sum 2. Comparing this with the definite integral gives an inequality that leads to e^15 < 2^32.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), notice the rectangle areas are 2^0, 2^(-1), 2^(-2), ... — a geometric series. For (b), integrate using 2^(-x) = e^(-x ln 2). For (c), compare the finite integral to the infinite rectangle sum since the integral lies below the rectangles.

steps.title: Rectangle areas form a geometric series
steps.bodyLatex: The nth rectangle (where x = n to n+1) has height 2^(-n), so its area is 1 × 2^(-n) = 2^(-n). This gives the series 1 + 1/2 + 1/4 + 1/8 + ... = sum of 2^(-n) for n = 0, 1, 2, ... . This is a geometric series with first term a = 1 and common ratio r = 1/2.

steps.title: Find the limiting sum
steps.bodyLatex: For a geometric series with |r| < 1, the limiting sum is a/(1 - r). Here a = 1 and r = 1/2, so the sum is 1/(1 - 1/2) = 1/(1/2) = 2. This confirms part (a).

steps.title: Evaluate the definite integral
steps.bodyLatex: Write 2^(-x) = e^(-x ln 2). Using ∫ e^(kx) dx = e^(kx)/k, we get ∫ 2^(-x) dx = -2^(-x)/ln 2. Evaluating from 0 to 4: [-2^(-x)/ln 2]₀⁴ = (-2^(-4) + 2^0)/ln 2 = (1 - 1/16)/ln 2 = 15/(16 ln 2). This confirms part (b).

steps.title: Compare integral to rectangle sum
steps.bodyLatex: The integral from 0 to 4 is less than the total area of all rectangles (since the rectangles extend from x = 0 to ∞ and the integral stops at x = 4). Thus 15/(16 ln 2) < 2. Multiplying: 15 < 32 ln 2. Exponentiating: e^15 < e^(32 ln 2) = (e^ln 2)^32 = 2^32. This completes part (c).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert 2^(-x) to e^(-x ln 2) when integrating, which changes the base and requires ln 2 in the denominator.
Not recognizing that the finite integral must be less than the infinite rectangle sum, then incorrectly comparing them as equal.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify numerically: e^15 ≈ 3.27 × 10^6 and 2^32 ≈ 4.29 × 10^9, so the inequality holds. The rectangle sum 2 equals 15/(16 ln 2) ≈ 1.36, confirming the integral is smaller.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question uses the CDF of a continuous random variable to find the constant k and then solve an equation involving probabilities.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that a CDF must satisfy F(e³) = 1 at the upper boundary, which directly gives k = 3. For part (b), express both probabilities in terms of F(c) and solve the given relationship.

steps.title: Find k from the CDF boundary
steps.bodyLatex: Since F is a cumulative distribution function, it must equal 1 at the upper limit of the domain. At x = e³, we have F(e³) = (1/k)·ln(e³) = (1/k)·3 = 1. Solving: 3/k = 1, so k = 3.

steps.title: Express the required probabilities
steps.bodyLatex: For a continuous random variable, P(X < c) = F(c) by definition of the CDF. Also P(X > c) = 1 - P(X ≤ c) = 1 - F(c), since the probability of X exactly equaling c is zero.

steps.title: Apply the given condition
steps.bodyLatex: The condition P(X < c) = 2P(X > c) becomes F(c) = 2(1 - F(c)). Rearranging: F(c) = 2 - 2F(c), so 3F(c) = 2, giving F(c) = 2/3.

steps.title: Solve for c
steps.bodyLatex: Since c lies in the middle region (1 ≤ c ≤ e³), use F(c) = (1/3)·ln c = 2/3. Multiplying by 3: ln c = 2, so c = e².

commonMistakesLatex: Using P(X > c) = 1 - F(c-) instead of 1 - F(c). For continuous random variables, this makes no difference since there's no point mass at c, but some students unnecessarily complicate this.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify: P(X < e²) = F(e²) = (1/3)·ln(e²) = 2/3, and P(X > e²) = 1 - 2/3 = 1/3. Indeed 2/3 = 2 × (1/3), confirming the answer.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: Use the intercept form of a line to find the relationship between the intercepts, then express the triangle area as a function of x and minimize using differentiation.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice that the line passes through (1,2) and has intercepts at (x,0) and (0,y). The intercept form x/a + y/b = 1 gives the key equation. Since x > 1, this restriction matters when finding the minimum.

steps.title: Set up using intercept form
steps.bodyLatex: A line intersecting the axes at $(x,0)$ and $(0,y)$ has equation $\n\frac{X}{x}+\frac{Y}{y}=1\)$. Since the line passes through $P(1,2)$, substitute these coordinates: $\frac{1}{x}+\frac{2}{y}=1\)$. This is the relationship connecting the intercepts.

steps.title: Solve for y in terms of x
steps.bodyLatex: From $\frac{1}{x}+\frac{2}{y}=1$, multiply by $xy$: $y+2x=xy$. Rearranging: $2x = xy - y = y(x-1)$, so $\n y = \frac{2x}{x-1}\)$. This holds for $x > 1$ (ensuring $y$ is positive and the point $X$ is to the right of $P$).

steps.title: Express area as function of x
steps.bodyLatex: Triangle $XOY$ has vertices at the origin and the intercepts, so its area is $A = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2}xy$. Substituting $y = \frac{2x}{x-1}$ gives $A(x) = \frac{1}{2}x \cdot \frac{2x}{x-1} = \frac{x^2}{x-1}\). This is the function to minimize.

steps.title: Differentiate and find minimum
steps.bodyLatex: Differentiate using the quotient rule: $A'(x) = \frac{2x(x-1) - x^2}{(x-1)^2} = \frac{x^2 - 2x}{(x-1)^2} = \frac{x(x-2)}{(x-1)^2}\)$. Setting $A'(x) = 0$ gives $x = 0$ or $x = 2$. Since $x > 1$, we take $x = 2$. Then $y = \frac{2(2)}{2-1} = 4$, and minimum area $A = \frac{1}{2}(2)(4) = 4$ square units.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: \(y = \frac{2x}{x-1}\). The minimum possible area of triangle $XOY$ is $4$ square units.
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the restriction $x > 1$ and incorrectly accepting $x = 0$ as a stationary point. Always check that your solution satisfies the domain conditions given in the problem.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the minimum by checking that $A''(2) > 0$: $A''(x)$ would be positive at $x = 2$, confirming a minimum rather than maximum. As a sanity check, sketch a triangle with base 2 and height 4 — its area is indeed 4 square units.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question involves a reducing-balance loan using geometric series. Part (a) requires solving for the monthly repayment when the loan is fully amortised. Parts (b) and (c) require finding the remaining balance after a rate change and calculating how many more repayments are needed.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice the geometric series formula in the model: the sum 1 + r + r² + ... + r^(n-1) equals (r^n - 1)/(r - 1). Set A_180 = 0 to find M, then calculate A_100 (the balance after 100 repayments), and use this as the principal for the new calculation with the altered interest rate. The final repayment is less than the regular payment because it includes only the remaining balance plus one month of interest.

steps.title: Find the monthly repayment M
steps.bodyLatex: Set A_{180} = 0 (loan fully repaid after 180 months). Using the geometric sum formula, the repayment sum is \(\frac{1.0025^{180}-1}{0.0025}\). Substituting: \[0=200000(1.0025)^{180}-M\cdot\frac{1.0025^{180}-1}{0.0025}\] Solving for M gives \[M=200000(1.0025)^{180}\cdot\frac{0.0025}{1.0025^{180}-1}\approx1381.16\] Using a calculator: \(1.0025^{180}\approx1.5674\), so \(M\approx200000\times1.5674\times0.0025/0.5674\approx\$1381.16\).

steps.title: Calculate balance after 100 repayments
steps.bodyLatex: Find A_{100} using the same formula with n = 100. First, \(1.0025^{100}\approx1.2837\). Then: \[A_{100}=200000(1.0025)^{100}-1381.16\cdot\frac{1.0025^{100}-1}{0.0025}\approx\$100032\] This is approximately $100,032 owing when the interest rate changes.

steps.title: Find number of full repayments at new rate
steps.bodyLatex: Now use the new monthly rate of 0.35% (r = 1.0035) with principal $100,032. Let n be the number of further repayments needed. Set \[100032(1.0035)^n-1381.16\cdot\frac{1.0035^n-1}{0.0035}=0\] Solving gives \(1.0035^n\approx1.3397\). Taking logs: \(n\cdot\ln(1.0035)=\ln(1.3397)\), so \(n\approx83.67\). Jane makes 83 full monthly repayments of $1,381.16.

steps.title: Calculate final repayment
steps.bodyLatex: After 83 repayments at the new rate, calculate the remaining balance. With \(1.0035^{83}\approx1.3363\): \[\text{Balance}=100032(1.0035)^{83}-1381.16\cdot\frac{1.0035^{83}-1}{0.0035}\approx\$928.29\] The final repayment pays this balance plus one month of interest: \(928.29\times1.0035\approx\$931.54\). This final payment is less than $1,381.16.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) M = $1,381.16 (b) 83 more full monthly repayments (c) Final repayment ≈ $931.54
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to use the new interest rate (0.35% = 1.0035) for part (b) calculations instead of the original 0.25%. Students may incorrectly continue using the original rate after the rate change occurs.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that 83 + 100 = 183 total repayments are reasonable (slightly more than the original 180 due to the higher interest rate). Also check that the final repayment ($931.54) is less than the regular payment ($1,381.16), which makes sense since it's a partial final payment.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: A horizontal shift right by $a$ units transforms $y=f(x)$ to $y=f(x-a)$.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Notice the shift direction. A right shift means points move to larger $x$-values, which requires subtracting from the input.

steps.title: Identify the transformation type
steps.bodyLatex: We have a horizontal shift 3 units to the RIGHT. This is a translation, not a stretch or reflection.

steps.title: Apply the horizontal shift rule
steps.bodyLatex: For a horizontal shift right by $a$ units, replace every $x$ with $(x-a)$. Here $a=3$, so $x$ becomes $x-3$.

steps.title: Write the transformed function
steps.bodyLatex: Substituting into the original $y=f(x)$ gives the transformed function $y=f(x-3)$.

steps.title: Verify with a test point
steps.bodyLatex: For a point $(c, f(c))$ on the original graph, after shifting right 3 units it becomes $(c+3, f(c))$. At $x=c+3$, we have $f((c+3)-3)=f(c)$, confirming the shift is correct.

commonMistakesLatex: Writing $f(x+3)$ instead of $f(x-3)$: remember that shifting RIGHT requires SUBTRACTING from the input, because points move to larger $x$-values.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Quick check: at $x=3$, the new function evaluates $f(0)$, which is the original value at $x=0$ — the point has moved 3 units right as required.
